macro_summary: Monday's session saw SPY edge up 0.2% to 745.64 as the market absorbed hawkish FOMC minutes and the aftermath of the Trump-Xi summit, which yielded no new semiconductor export control changes but left US-China trade tensions unresolved. Energy markets slipped, with WTI retreating 0.9% to $96.60 despite an impasse over reopening the Strait of Hormuz and Bloomberg reporting that war-driven inflation will be seen in the Fed's preferred gauge. Singapore's Q1 GDP was revised up to 6.0% y/y (per Reuters, May 25), yet local banks remain overbought and blocked. US10Y eased 3bp to 4.56%, while SOXX surged another 2.1% to 537.33, driven by AMD's $10B Taiwan AI investment, but internal divergence remains extreme.
commodities_note: WTI slipped to $96.60 (-0.9%) on mixed Hormuz signals; managed disruption keeps the floor near $100. Gold flat at $4,523. VIX at 16.7 remains below the 30 ACCUMULATE gate but the volatility compression is fragile given the oil supply backdrop.

accumulate_gates.note: All mechanical gates PASS, but momentum_warn is True (weak recent tape). Generic fundamental praise does NOT clear the bar — default to WATCH if neither anchor applies.

singapore.summary: All three Singapore banks remain hard-blocked on overbought RSI (DBS 80, OCBC 73.5, UOB 72.7) with volume fading. UOB is the closest to a workable entry at +3.9% above its 50-day, while DBS/OCBC need a meaningful pullback. The structural headwind persists: US10Y at 4.56% is still above the 4.20% threshold for NIM relief, and MAS S$NEER policy doesn't ease until October 2026. Singapore Q1 GDP growth revised to 6.0% (per Reuters, May 25) is positive but hasn't shifted the rate-driven rate-block on the banks.
singapore.thesis_status: Structurally cautious — overbought with no rate relief catalyst until September FOMC or October MAS.
singapore.key_development: Singapore Q1 GDP revised to 6.0% y/y (per Reuters, May 25) above advance estimate, reinforcing economic resilience, but US10Y at 4.56% remains above the 4.20% NIM reset threshold, keeping banks blocked.

semiconductors.summary: SOXX surged another 2.1% to 537.33 (+21.8% over the month) driven by AMD's $10B Taiwan investment, but internal divergence is extreme: AMD +54% above SMA50, INTC +57%, MU +45%, 000660.KS +55%, while NVDA -8.1% vs cluster this week and AVGO -42.2% vs cluster over the month. Eight names face >20% extreme extension blocks; TSM and NVDA are the least extended but still blocked. Calibration lessons indicate CAUTION frequently precedes further rallies (AMD 9/9, MU 9/9, INTC 9/10), warning against short-selling, but the extension blocks are absolute — no entries until pullbacks materialise.
semiconductors.thesis_status: Structurally bullish, tactically overbought — sector beta is the dominant driver, not stock selection.
semiconductors.key_development: AMD's $10B Taiwan investment (per Simply Wall St./Insider Monkey, May 23) and TSEM's $1.3B photonics contracts (per Simply Wall St., May 24) underscore AI capex momentum, but divergence between winners and laggards within the cluster is at extreme levels.

big_tech.summary: BigTech is mixed: MSFT is a HOLD near entry (+4.7% above SMA50, R:R 0.8:1 with tight upside), AMZN and GOOG are extended at +10.1% and +12.0% above their 50-days, and PLTR remains CAUTION on a 66x forward PE despite defense AI momentum. Berkshire's full exit of Amazon (per Simply Wall St., May 24) adds a contrarian signal, but the structural capex theme remains intact. MSFT's non-OpenAI RPO (+28%) is the cleanest demand signal in the group and the next Azure catalyst.
big_tech.thesis_status: Structurally bullish, tactically asymmetric — MSFT is the only name near entry, others are extended or overpriced.
big_tech.key_development: Berkshire Hathaway exited Amazon completely (per Simply Wall St., May 24) while building a top-5 position in Alphabet; AI capex still the backbone, but stock selection within BigTech is polarising.

interconnected.name: Coherent Corp. (COHR)
interconnected.reason: NVDA $2B photonics investment partner alongside LITE — optical interconnect demand for AI clusters links directly to NVDA GPU shipments and LITE's Greensboro fab buildout.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if LITE's Greensboro fab production milestones are confirmed and COHR pulls back 8-10% from current levels.

interconnected.name: Nebius Group (NBIS)
interconnected.reason: Signed a $2.6B, 10-year fuel cell deal with Bloom Energy (BE) — NBIS's AI data center power demand directly benefits BE's revenue, making NBIS a leading indicator for BE's power-infra thesis.
interconnected.entry_note: Monitor BE news for NBIS contract milestones; if BE posts strong earnings, NBIS's power demand is validated. Add to watchlist if NBIS corrects 10% from recent highs.

interconnected.name: Arista Networks (ANET)
interconnected.reason: AI datacenter networking upgrades benefit Arista from hyperscaler AI capex — NOK's Spectrum-X and 800G optical transceiver ramp share the same hyperscaler switch spend, making ANET a correlated read-through.
interconnected.entry_note: Revisit after NOK earnings on July 23 for confirmation of 800G switch revenues; if NOK's AI networking segment accelerates, ANET becomes a watchlist candidate.

interconnected.name: Tesla (TSLA)
interconnected.reason: Tesla's confirmed role as Intel 14A's first external customer (April 2026) directly validates Intel's foundry thesis, and Tesla's FSD chip demand could influence TSMC's CoWoS capacity allocation, impacting TSM's order book.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if Intel announces additional 14A design wins beyond Tesla, confirming foundry momentum, and TSLA pulls back 10%.

active_risks: Strait of Hormuz: Tensions remain high with impasse over reopening; oil infrastructure damage may limit production beyond 2026, keeping oil prices elevated (WTI $96.60, Brent $104.4) and sustaining energy inflation (per Bloomberg, Fortune, May 23-24).

US-China tariff/semiconductor tensions: Aftermath of Trump-Xi summit leaves semiconductor export controls unchanged, but China's retaliatory tariffs cost US ag exporters $15B and rare earth/mineral supply chains remain contested (per NPR, Successful Farming, May 22).

Fed rate hike risks and inflation: Hawkish FOMC minutes show majority support rate hikes if Hormuz-driven inflation persists; consumer long-term inflation expectations unanchor, pushing US10Y to 4.56% and diminishing rate-cut expectations for 2026 (per Fortune, Crux Investor, May 23).

ECB recession divergence: ECB 'hell-bent' on rate hikes despite recession risks in Germany, France, Italy, widening the transatlantic policy divergence and adding global uncertainty (per CNBC, May 22).
geopolitical.market_impact: Elevated energy costs sustain inflation, keeping rate-hike expectations alive and capping PE multiples — semis and BigTech remain extended but face no direct sell-off, while Singapore banks are blocked by overbought signals. The Hormuz impasse directly benefits defense names like PLTR but limits broad market entry opportunities.
geopolitical.new_since_yesterday: Bloomberg reports that war-driven inflation will be seen in the Fed's favored PCE gauge this month; U.S. manufacturing PMI rose to a four-year high of 55.3 in May on inventory building due to Iran war supply worries (per Reuters, May 21). ECB criticism of rate hikes intensifies.
geopolitical.portfolio_action: Hold cash, no new entries across the board — 17 of 25 names are hard-blocked on extreme extension, and the few near-entry setups (MSFT, CRWV, WRD) carry either tight upside or thin R:R; wait for VIX to reprice geopolitical risks or for specific pullbacks to clear the gates.

portfolio_snapshot.overall_stance: Defensive — extreme extension dominates all clusters
portfolio_snapshot.risk_posture: 17 of 25 names are hard-blocked on technical extension, and even relative-value names (MSFT, CRWV) lack clean entry triggers; the dominant risk is a sudden oil-driven inflation spike triggering a VIX surge that would accelerate profit-taking in overbought names.
